activity_metadata.objective: Students will analyze how a particular stanza fits into the overall structure of "I, Too" and contributes to the development of theme.
activity_metadata.alignment_long: This activity replaces the "Your Turn" segment from the StudySync Poetic Elements and Structure Skills lesson for "I, Too." Students analyze how line breaks, stanza length, and open form structure fit into the overall structure of the poem and contribute to the development of theme.
activity_metadata.connection_to_end_of_unit_assessment_tas: This activity directly supports the Lesson 16 Literary Analysis task where students will analyze how Langston Hughes uses poetic elements and structure to explore the theme of change in "I, Too." By analyzing how line breaks create pace, how varying stanza lengths affect rhythm, and how open form structure relates to the poem's message, students develop the foundational analytical skills they will need to write about the effect of poetic structure on theme in their literary analysis response.
activity_metadata.unit_level_connection: This task connects to the unit's essential question: "How do we know what to do when there are no instructions?" The speaker in "I, Too" faces a situation without clear instructions—he is excluded and sent to the kitchen, yet he demonstrates confidence and hope that change will come. By analyzing how Hughes structures the poem to convey this message of hope and self-assurance, students explore how individuals find direction and strength even when facing uncertainty and injustice. The poem's open form structure itself mirrors the theme of navigating without rigid guidelines, as Hughes breaks free from traditional poetic constraints to express the speaker's vision of equality.
activity_metadata.excerpt:
I, Too

I, too, sing America.

I am the darker brother.

They send me to eat in the kitchen

When company comes,

(5) But I laugh,

And eat well,

And grow strong.

Tomorrow,

I'll be at the table

(10) When company comes.

Nobody'll dare

Say to me,

"Eat in the kitchen,"

Then.

(15) Besides,

They'll see how beautiful I am

And be ashamed –

I, too, am America.
